speaker: interviewer
text: The trial-- were are you allowed to sit in the trial during all the witnesses' testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any time that you were not allowed into the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All the time when I wanted. I have my pass. I'll show you. You can take a picture of that right now. And I could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, we can do that later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't, don't-- you don't need to concern yourself with that now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I have it elsewhere. Yeah, you Yeah, you have it there, yeah, OK. All right, I show you my pass of the-- All right, when-- did you testify the first day of the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which day did you testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot recall that, what day it was. It was so long going on, maybe in, in the second week or maybe even the third week.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. You were talking about the French journalist--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who came to your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was-- he came into my room. And he had the little packets, as I told you. And he says, I want to give you a gift. I said, I appreciate it very much. And he took out his suit. He said, that's-- I want feel that there's-- like, I knew somebody, and I did something. I said, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he said, can I come in one day and talk to you? So I told him, you can come even tonight. Arrange with the MP by the door, with the-- with the guards. And I cannot tell you on that. You've got to get a permission from the-- from the guards, from the security. So he must have got a pass or a permit. And he came in. And he was sitting with me. And he was asking me where I was born and what my father was doing and everything. And he wrote pages and pages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, I don't know how to thank you. The only thing I can say-- that in the near future, your picture is going to be in Paris and all over in the papers. I said, thank you very much. [LAUGHS] OK. And, and he left. But I never used my head to ask him to send me a newspaper or something like that, you know. You're-- so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in strain all the time that you miss out a lot. Then later, you think, one word I would have told him, I could have had it. OK, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau? Yeah, I was safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt safe, even--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was so safe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that sometime a colonel who was involved, you know, with that trial [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said to me, Moses, can I wake you up one time around 1 o'clock or 12 o'clock at night? I want to show you something that you are going to be very happy. And it'll be your memory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said that he had a permission, if I say yes, to take me. I says, if it's going to be my memory for good, you can take me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, he knocked on the door. And he says, Moses, I'll take you right where all the 40 prisoners are sleeping [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in their circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And through a window, you know, I can hear everything what they say, something like that, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can see them. But I don't think they can see me, something like that it was. And I could hear what they're talking. And I heard that the-- all the 40 criminals were fighting with each other, not in physically fight, but mouth, cursing each other. But you were the main chief! You told me to do that! So I did it! And that was saying, you were higher than me! I didn't want to do it. You forced me to do! And I heard terrible things, you know, between the 40 criminals who were big shots before, that to kill a man, a human being for them is like stepping on a-- on an ant. And then I see them all of a sudden like babies because they were fighting. And I was standing there and listening everything. And one was blaming the other, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the room look like that they were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like a empty, actually, nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there beds for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see no beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in their uniforms, but all jalopied, you know, not a shiny, glittering things. You're [? sure, ?] you know, like prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think this is the room they slept in or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they were kept--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't kept in that room.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were ordered, maybe, at that time especially for me, [LAUGHS] to come in in that room, you understand, and to stay there, maybe for three or four hours. But that American was a high-ranked man. He could give all kinds of orders. And maybe that's what happened. He gave the order. And I was-- I was in heaven. That was, for me, a very [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] powerful, you know, for my heart, you know, a feeling to see that. Because memories came to my mind-- all the people what they were killed and the wishes they had while they were dying. And I-- I'm right here, and I am living it-- through that, you know. So it was morally, for me, such a accomplishment and such a-- I must say again that bad word, 'revenge.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you were treated differently than the other witnesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: I may be because I was the youngest. Like, I was, like, to say a [NON-ENGLISH], you see. But most of them were like older people, witnesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you restricted in, in where you could go and things you could do during the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I couldn't go outside, you know. But inside, in the-- in the place, in Dachau in the yard, all over, I could go. I wasn't afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you restricted in who you could talk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But there was all the time in the yard all kind of, believe me, securities or FBI or CIA, who knows what? It was all the time people around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk to other witnesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were not told not to discuss anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I was not, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. In the-- in the trial itself, can you name the defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can name my people who I knew and what I was going to testify against them. And before I was going to testify, I was given orders and a law to obey. And the law and the order to obey was when I'm called on the stand and I'm called in to show and to recognize and to make sure that that's the prisoner I'm testifying against, I'm allowed to stay-- to go up on the stand, like a tribune, to him and stay away from him 12 inches. [POINTING] With my finger away from him 12 inches, that's what I'm allowed to do. I cannot do-- no closer go to him, or no violent moves to make on him, and to be restrained, and keep my temper at all time. I was told that. But, to regret, it didn't turn out that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was called to the stand. And I was, you, Morris-- you, Moses Rutschaisky, swear to God nothing but the truth to tell so-- God-- so help you God? I do. They start to ask me the questions. What did you see? What happened when you came to Dachau? I says, when I came to Dachau, we were taken off the trains. And by the train was already the commandant. Where's the-- what's his name? Johann Kirsch, he came to greet us by the train. What happened then? Then we were all stand up, six in a row. And we were marched to the camp. Then, when we came to the camp, the gates opened up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the gates opened up, we were no more humans. We were animals. We were chased. Sticks of wood, bricks-- everything was flying over our heads. And shots in the air was. And then we had to stand what it's called the 'Appellplatz,' where you were counted, you know, and stand still, not to move. And all the prisoners have to stand still. And then the commandant stepped up on the little stepping bench, which was, maybe, about 18 inches tall, with his green SS uniform, with his red cheeks-- an older man, maybe about 55 years old, oh, at least 50. And he stood-- I see him now-- and he stood with his hand like Napoleon. His hand was right there, like Napoleon. And he gave a hollow, [SPEAKING GERMAN]! That means everybody to be in 100%, you know, quietness. And he was standing still with his hand. And he start making speech. And I had to tell all this to that prosecutor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his speech? he asked me. I said, his speech was-- his first words was, don't forget for a minute you are all in Dachau. You are nothings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he used a very bad word, which he was using all the time since we knew him in the camp. Maybe I should tell you now in the camera or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you are [GERMAN]. People who understand that the world knows what it means. So we gave him a name-- all the prisoners gave him a name. That's what he called us. We called him, here goes the [GERMAN]. It's a bad name, bad word, not intelligent. He says, you are now in Dachau. And don't forget it for a minute. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And don't think that Germany is going to lose the war. Because, you know, it was already done. It was in 1944. It was going-- the battle was going down. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And don't forget, he says, that Deutschland is going kaput-- that Germany is going to be finished. And if [SPEAKING GERMAN], kaput, the whole world will go kaput. The whole world will be finished. And don't forget it. And he was sarcastic. And he said, your President Rosenberg-- he didn't want to mention 'Roosevelt.' Your President Roosevelt-- Rosenberg knows it very well that we have a certain gift for him. And he's going to get it. Because they were all talked into the atom bomb, which they were working on it all the time. It's going to be ready any day. And after the speech, he says, anybody who is not going to behave or who is going to do things against the rules of the camp, he's going to be shot on the spot. And now, you're all a list. And you're going to go to give away all your private things, your private clothes. You're all prisoners of Dachau. And you're going to be numbered. And loose! And we were all chased away. And he walked away. And then the other Germans took over the work, take away the clothes, you know, being watched, you know, observed, looked for things, pictures, everything you had to give away. And then they start to throw you the clothes, you know, the German prisoner clothes. Tall people grabbed, you know, clothes which is for short men. Short men grabbed clothes what for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --long men. We all looked like clowns-- terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, I want to ask you some more things about the trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes. And then, the prosecutor asked me, did you see terrible things what the commandant did to people? I says, yes, I've seen with my own eyes. We came from the work one day. And the law was that before we come to the gate, we all have to take off the hat-- the, the hat. And a German SS stood by the gate. And he was hollering, give all the time that order, [SPEAKING GERMAN]! That means they've got to take off their hats. And one of my friend [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] didn't take off the hat. He forgot in the minute to take off the hat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Johann Kirsch noticed that. He dragged him out from the line. He knocked him on the floor. And with his boots, he trampled onto him. And he knocked him in the head so much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he killed the man. He was dead. He didn't move. All blood was-- he was all smashed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then one German-- one American, you know, who defender-- who defends them, you know. It works in a court-- prosecutor and defender, one colonel. The defender stood up. And he said, how do you know he was dead? I says, because I saw, and I heard. When the doctor-- the German doctor came out, and he looked at him. He says that he's finished, that he's dead, kaput. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said-- and I said at the time what it was. He says, how do you this is the time? You were take-- when you came to the camp, you were undressed and taken away everything. You didn't have a watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I know the time because the whistle blew. And it was to get coffee, the water coffee. That's why I know the time. So the defender sat down. And he didn't find any points, you know, for Johann Kirsch. So that was the man who I saw with my own eyes he killed. His name was Moishe [? Jablonski. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, excuse me. Excuse me. Moishe Baliban, his name was Moishe Baliban. He was a friend of mine from my hometown. He says, what kind of incident more did you see? I saw a terrible incident-- disgrace. He never allowed himself to called-- the eldest from the Jews from the prisoners who was the eldest in the ghetto, and then he was in the camp, Dr. Elkis-- he didn't allow himself to call him 'Doctor.' When he want, just slap him in the face. He was an old man. Dr. Elkis was almost, like, 70 years old. He slapped him in the face. And he called him [GERMAN]. He didn't want to give him even that word, the title doctor. So he called him [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means 'bonebreaker.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he called him in the room while we were inside, in the barracks. And he says to the old man, a 70-year-old man, go on the bed and dance for me a little while he was slapped in the face and beaten. A prominent doctor like Dr. Elkis, he made him dance in the bed. And after he was hardly moving his leg, he gave him such a slap in his face that the man was maybe knocked out for days. And after he left the barrack from the doctor, he went around in the yard, and whoever he could kill with his feet, that man was killing-- all of it his boots-- people. Whoever was close by, he knocked them right with the boot in the head. And in the right place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That man single-handed-- single foot-- killed dozens of people laying dead on the ground. And that was Johann Kirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the defense attorney asking about these incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't ask me anything else no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Kirsch in the courtroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Johann Kirsch was right on the thing. And then, after that I was [INAUDIBLE] out. But then he says, do you really know Johann Kirsch? Maybe you just heard about him stories. I wonder you should come to Johann Kirsch on the stage there where they are placed and show where is Johann Kirsch. Please, Mr. Moses, point out where Johann Kirsch is. And, when I came to point out where Johann Kirsch, I forgot about the law. And I forgot how far I got to stay away from him. My nerves got ahold of me. And instead of to show with the finger, I jumped on his neck with my hands. And I says, this is the murderer! This is Johann Kirsch! And in the court right away with the gavel, the main prosecutor knocked to-- with the gavel in court. And the court became with the cameras, with the feeling, with all the things right on the prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the prosecutor with the gavel start to stop the commotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, Mr. Moses Rutschaisky, if you are going to be-- behave like that, you are going to be taken out of, of the courtroom. I don't want to see no more action like that and disturbances. Understood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm sorry. I'm sorry very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I apologize. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he quiet down the court. And all the cameras from all over-- the journalists, they had all the cameras on that instance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Kirsch's reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kirsch's reaction? When I come to testify, the law was he had to stand up for me. And that was, for me, the biggest things in my life, that that murderer-- that a human being was, for him, nothing. You don't know how many people he killed, he tortured. He hung people in the prison in Dachau. He hung seven people, seven boys. And the whole camp had to stay and watch. And the fact is, one of the boys' mother was working in the kitchen in the camp. She had to come out from the kitchen, too-- all the women. And she had to see her son being hung.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for what? The boys-- when we were going to work in the wintertime, it was so cold. And we were in thin, little shirts, in these striped shirts. And it was wet snow and rain. And we were working night shift. And we were trembling like that. So we were working in the cement place where we were pouring cement. So some of the boys took the empty cement bags. And under their shirt, they covered themselves with a string. And cement-- empty cement bags keeps, like, a little warm, almost like a sweater.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't take off the cement bags to throw away. They marched in with the cement bags in the camp. And somebody, maybe from the guards or somebody, squealed to the commandant. And they had to stay all on the-- on this Appellplatz, you know, on the place. And we were all touched. And they found the cement bags by seven boys. And that's why the seven boys was hung. And the whole camp had to stay on 'still stand' and look at the seven boys who were hung. And one of the boy's mother was fainting right near the kitchen. She had to see her boy being hung. And it was all directed by that Johann Kirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have to stand up during your entire testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only when I was pointing to make sure that the court sees that I know who I'm talking about. Then he was sitting down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all of the defendants in the trial all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. They were all sitting like in Nuremberg, exactly the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all sitting on tribunes. And they all had numbers, 1, 2, 3, up to 40. It was 40 criminals. And 36 were hung by the neck until dead. Only one-- as they all had a last word, and one-- a lot of people-- they ask if they are guilty. Nicht schuldig, nicht schuldig, 'not guilty.' And one didn't say, not schuldig. That was Kramer. Kramer was the man who was-- who liquidated the ghetto of Warsaw. The Warsaw Ghetto, he was from the liquidators. And when he was told, you know, his fate, he says-- he hollered, Heil Hitler! I hated the Jews all the time! I hate them now! And I'll hate them until I go to my grave. He's the only one who made that speech. The other prosecutors-- the other defendants tried to say they had orders, like everybody. The man I was prosecuting, that Moll, who beat me almost to death, he came into my barrack where I was working. And I had a potato, a big potato, boiling in a pot of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saw that big potato. He looked at it, says, whose potato is that? I says, it's mine. He says, where did you get it? I didn't want to tell him where I got it. Because one German from the [INAUDIBLE] there, where I was working in the barrack-- remember, while I was working already inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a French Volksdeutsche. He's a German, but he lives all his life in France. He had kind of mercy on me. He was not a SS man. He was just, you know, a mechanic, a German mechanic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he used to bring me all the time in a bag the crust of the bread, other little food, you know, crumbs. And he put it away near the barrack. And I knew that's for me. And I used to pick it up. And I used to throw it in a metal can with water and cook it to make a bread soup. One day, he brought me a potato like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my bad luck, I was cooking the potato. And there walked in Sturmfuhrer Moll. And he sees the potato. And he says, we Germans don't have that kind of big, beautiful potatoes. Where did you get that potato?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I found the potato outside in-- near the barrack. Du liegst, he says, you are lying. And he took a broomstick handle. In Europe, they have the old-fashioned brooms, you know, like, made, you know, from weeds, you know. He pulled out-- he pulled out that broom-handle stick. And he was beating me so much that I didn't feel-- I didn't feel my body. I didn't feel nothing. I was two weeks, like, paralyzed, laying in the-- laying in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop--

speaker: interviewee
text: He beat me almost to death for that potato. So when it came to court-- and I told the story about that. And they asked him, he says, yes, I remember. He admitted. He admitted that he beated me almost to death. He wanted to beat me to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we need to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he thought I was--
